ISEs Solid-State Electrodes – The Fluoride Electrode (1 of 2)
Fluoride ISE
Figure 15-23
Fluoride ISEs are used to monitor the fluoride content of municipal water supplies.
In a fluoride ISE, a LaF 3 crystal doped with a small amount of Eu 2+ serves as the ion selective membrane.
Figure 15-24
The Eu 2+ creates fluoride vacancies in the crystal that enable a tiny current of fluoride ions to move through the LaF 3 crystal

Liquid-Based Ion-Selective Electrodes
Calcium ion-selective electrode with a liquid ion exchanger
The ion-selective membrane consists of a hydrophobic polymer membrane impregnated with a hydrophobic ionophore that is selective for Ca 2+ .

A pH electrode for Mars – A liquid-based ISE
The Phoenix Mars Lander carried liquid-based ion-selective electrodes to study the pH of the soil on Mars.
The ionophore in the liquid-based ISE pH probes was ETH 2418
measuring range: 1 < pH < 9
selectivity coefficients:

Compound Electrodes (1 of 2)
Compound electrodes contain a conventional electrode surrounded by a membrane that isolates (or generates) the analyte to which the electrode responds.
The Severinghaus CO 2 sensing compound electrode
When CO 2 diffuses through the semipermeable membrane, it reacts with water to form carbonic acid.
Carbonic acid lowers the pH of the thin layer of electrolyte inside the CO 2 permeable membrane.
The glass electrode responds to the change in pH, providing a measure of the CO 2 concentration outside the electrode.
Figure 15-33

Compound Electrodes (2 of 2)
The Severinghaus CO 2 sensing compound electrode also detects other acidic or basic gases, including
NH 3
SO 2
H 2 S
NO x (nitrogen oxides)
HN 3 ( hydrazoic acid)
If present, other acidic or basic gases will interfere with the CO 2 measurement.
Figure 15-33

Advantages of Ion-Selective Electrodes
less expensive than competing techniques such as atomic spectroscopy and ion chromatography
linear response to log
over a wide range
nondestructive
noncontaminating
short response time
unaffected by color or turbidity

Challenges of Ion-Selective Electrodes
Precision is usually worse than 1%
Electrodes can be fouled by proteins or other organic solutes  sluggish, drifting response
Certain ions interfere with or poison particular electrodes
Some electrodes are fragile and have limited shelf life
Reliable concentration measurements require the ionic strength to be held constant in all matrices
Electrodes respond only to uncomplexed ions. Complexed ions are not seen.

Detecting Ca 2+ in human plasma
Much of the Ca 2+ in human plasma is complexed by other ligands, and thus is invisible to an ISE based sensor.
Other measurement approaches can be used to see all of the Ca 2+ (free and complexed) in the plasma.
For Example: Capillary Electrophoresis coupled to an Inductively Coupled Plasma Detector (ICP)
The largest peak is free Ca 2+ . The other peaks are proteins or small molecules to which Ca 2+ is bound.
Detection of calcium-containing species in human blood plasma by capillary electrophoresis-ICP.
Figure 15-35

Using Standard Additions with Electrodes (1 of 2)
When ion-selective electrodes are used, it is important that the composition of the standard solution closely approximates the composition of the unknown.
This can be ensured by using the method of standard additions .
In the method of standard additions, the electrode is immersed in a sample of the unknown and the potential is recorded before and after each small aliquot of standard solution is added.
A graphical procedure is used to extrapolate back to the volume of standard ( V s ) that would contain the same number of moles of analyte as the unknown.
Figure 15-36

Solid-State Chemical Sensors
Built from semiconductors (Si, Ge, GaAs, etc.)
Contain both an indicator and a reference electrode
More rugged than ion-selective electrodes
Smaller sensing area than ion-selective electrodes
Figure 15-38
Figure 15-37

Doping Impurities Increase Conductivity
Figure 15-39
( a ) pure silicon: electrons are locked into sigma bonds.
( b ) n -doped silicon: the impurity (e.g. P) increases conductivity by adding one extra electron(•) per P, which is relatively free to move through the crystal.
( c ) p -doped silicon: the impurity (e.g. Al) increases conductivity by adding a hole (o). The new hole can be occupied by an electron from a neighboring bond, effectively moving the hole to the neighboring bond.
